_comment: Probe manifest (docs/02 §4): the editable knob that drives `update-offsets` offset
re-discovery. It describes what is provably TRUE at the fixed calibration setup — practice
mode, P1 Jin vs P2 Kazuya, round start — so calibration after a patch is a DATA EDIT here,
not a source change. Everything here is FACTS/DATA (known values, plausibility bounds, scan
windows), not copyrightable expression (docs/02 §5). Extra keys like this comment are ignored.
The scan windows are module-relative placeholders aligned to the C4a placeholder table; they
WILL need calibration on the real build (widen/relocate, or set `absolute` and point at an
address you located — player structs behind a pointer chain are not reachable by a
module-relative window). See the runbook printed by `update-offsets`.
notes: Default manifest — calibrate scan windows on the real build (docs/02 §4).
_action_window_comment: C4g: the behavioral oracles observe a TIMED WINDOW, not two instants. move_id is transient —
a jab or a jump rewrites it for about half a second and the character then idles back to the
value it held at round start. C4f asked the user to alt-tab and press Enter mid-jump, which no
human can do, so the real struct read as frozen at both ends and was rejected: the live scan
found ZERO behaving candidates. Now the tool prompts, waits `action_lead_in_seconds` for the
alt-tab, then samples every `action_sample_interval` for `action_window_seconds` and accepts a
struct whose move_id changed in ANY sample. Widen the window if the action feels rushed; the
cost is only sweep time. The window's measured duration also bounds how far a real frame
_base_scan_comment: C4d code-signature base derivation (docs/02 §3): locate the HEAP-allocated player struct by
scanning the module's static data for the pointer that leads to it, follow the pointer chain,
validate the landing against the known within-struct field layout, and then require the acting
player's move_id to CHANGE from its round-start value in at least one sample of the action
window (the BEHAVIORAL half of the oracle, C4f/C4g: a coincidental struct reads plausibly at
round start but does not move when the player does; one was accepted live on 5.02.01 with
move_id frozen at 0 while Jin jumped). `max_strong_candidates` bounds how many
structurally-plausible landings are carried into that confirmation. `char_id_min` is 0: Jin's
real id may BE 0 on this build, and a zeroed page can never become a STRONG candidate anyway
(nothing in it reads Kazuya's 12 at a constant stride) nor pass the behavioral test. All values
here are FACTS/DATA (docs/02 §5): the within-struct field offsets and the stable-ish chain shape
were SEEDED from the community-known Tekken 8 layout (dcep93/TekkenBot's committed
memory_address.ini) — NOT the fork's update_memory_address.py script. `base_offset` (the static
slot) is NOT seeded: it shifts every build and is re-derived by the scan.
`pointer_path` = module+base_offset -> deref +0x10 -> +0x68 -> +0x8 -> +0x30. round_start_health
is full HP (200, confirmed live). The struct has no direct HP field, so health is computed as
round_start_health - damage_taken (the fork's model). struct_span is the in-struct health/position
scan bound; it is capped to the derived stride at runtime, so setting it >= max_stride just means
'scan the whole player struct'. These are durable; the runbook covers what to recalibrate.
`state_fields` (C4e): Tekken 8 stores ENCODED state words, not the per-flag booleans our schema
assumed. These offsets are facts/data from the same layout source; their decimal values are
move_frame 0x370, counter_state (hit_outcome) 0x5f0, recovery_state 0x5b4, simple_move_state
0x640, stun_type 0x644, throw_tech_state 0x668, complex_move_state 0x68c. The scalar KINDS are
hypotheses (u32 for the 4-aligned words) — calibrate if a field reads implausibly. The VALUE
semantics live in state-map.json and are calibrated by observation (docs/02 §8): no round-start
two-snapshot oracle can prove what stun_type==3 means, because nobody is in stun at round start.
The layout also carries attack_type at 0x4f1 (u8, high/mid/low); PlayerFrame has no home for it,
so it is deliberately not read.
`legacy_state_fields` are the C4a placeholder's per-flag booleans. They describe a struct that
does not exist on the real build, so the derivation DROPS them from the table it writes rather
than carrying forward offsets that would look like they work.
`component_scan` (C4e): position is NOT in the entity struct — a full-struct scan across a
walking snapshot pair finds no moving float triple, and the layout data has no position field.
It lives in a separate Unreal transform component the entity points at, so the scan sweeps the
entity's own pointer slots and looks for the moving (x,y,z) triple inside the pointee.
_global_scan_comment: C4e global/match anchor derivation (docs/02 §3). The global struct is heap-allocated behind its
own static pointer, exactly like the player struct, so the anchor is re-derived per build. What
is seeded is FACTS/DATA: the chain shape and the within-struct offsets known to carry match
state. The layout source records the frame counter as the offset run
but does NOT say where the pointer chain ends and the field offsets begin. So `pointer_paths` is
a LIST of candidate chain shapes, tried longest-first; the behavioral oracle rejects the wrong
ones, which is exactly what makes guessing here safe. [0] and [] cover the degenerate cases (a
single deref, and a global struct sitting directly at the slot).
`field_offsets` is deliberately UNASSIGNED: 0xd260/0xd2d8/0xd4a8 (53856/53976/54440) are known
to carry frame counter, round, timer and phase, but not in a known order. The scan assigns them
BY BEHAVIOR across two snapshots — one ticks up, one holds a round number, one counts down —
which is both clean-room and robust to the source data being reordered. Practice mode usually
freezes the round clock, so timer_ms is often unassignable; frame_counter + round is what the
oracle requires. Add offsets here rather than assigning them by hand.
`frame_delta_min`/`frame_delta_max` are the FALLBACK bounds, used when the caller cannot say how
long the two readings were apart: 36000 frames = 10 minutes at 60fps, so a u32 that jumped
further is a hash or a byte count. The live path CAN say — it times its own sampling window and
re-reads the before-values as the window opens — so it narrows the accept band to
`frame_rate * seconds`, give or take `frame_delta_tolerance` (C4g Phase 3). That is the real
discriminator: the user's run-1 saw a candidate tick up by 1 across the prompt and run-2 saw 96;
both passed the unbanded test, and 22-28 landings were accepted. `match_phase` is unassignable at
round start too; when it is, decode_frame reports match_state='unknown' and CAPTURE REFUSES to
record, while `doctor` still validates the anchors. game_mode is never derivable here (the mode
does not change while the user takes a step) — both are calibrated by hand, docs/02 §4.
The layout source also carries a static `opponent_side` at 0x9ad7060 +0x4. Static offsets like
that shift every build (that is why base_offset is re-derived), so it is recorded here as a fact
and not used.
_derive_scan_comment: it seeds NO within-struct offsets and NO pointer chain — on build 5.02.01 those seeds are stale
(the fork died Oct 2024) and a fair windowed run found 0 of 13 candidates behaving. It locates the
entity struct by BEHAVIOR: sweep the enumerated HEAP for Kazuya's id 12 beside a plausible id at a
constant stride whose surrounding struct reads SIMILAR (both players idle share many non-zero
words); confirm the acting player's move_id CHANGES across the action window; then reverse-scan the
static data for a pointer path to it and keep only paths that survive a REALLOCATION (a round
reset). char_id/move_id/stride/Jin's id/damage_taken and the pointer path are all OUTPUTS. The only
retained seed is our own C1 fact kazuya_char_id=12. Everything here is a tractability BOUND, not a
layout fact (the facts — Kazuya's id, char/move ranges, stride window, scan align — are the
manifest's existing top-level fields). Position/global/state reuse base_scan.component_scan /
global_scan / base_scan.state_fields (those are DATA facts): the encoded state-word OFFSETS remain
best-effort seeds, translated onto the derived base and flagged for docs/02 §8 re-derivation, since
no round-start oracle can prove where stun_type lives. `struct_span` bounds the differential/field
scans; `similarity_min`/`min_shared_words` are the zero-immune struct-similarity discriminator;
`reverse_max_depth`/`reverse_max_offset` bound the backward BFS; the rest are caps.
_holder_scan_comment: C4i holder derivation (docs/02 §3): `update-offsets --holder-scan`. Adopts the model the LIVE
T8 game actually uses, per three independent current community tools (tomislav-ivankovic/Irony,
sirmammingtonham/opendojo — verified live on v3.00.02 —, ParadiseAigo/Tekken-8-Frames-To-Speech).
The players are NOT a single-anchor + stride array: they hang off a HOLDER object by two
per-player pointer slots to SEPARATE allocations, and the holder's own .data slot is found by an
AoB CODE signature in .text with a RIP-relative displacement. Neither --base-scan (its stride
validation cannot express two separate allocations) nor --derive can express this. Everything
here is FACTS/DATA (docs/02 §5), usable with attribution — do NOT copy the tools' code.
`aob_pattern` matches the storing instruction MOV [rip+disp32], r14 ; MOV [r14+0x28], BL; the
disp32 is wildcarded (4 bytes at `disp32_pos` = 3). The slot RVA is match + disp32_pos + 4 +
disp32 (x64 RIP = next instruction). `holder_slots` [48, 56] = 0x30 (P1), 0x38 (P2). The oracle
fields are the SAME community offsets --base-scan uses: char_id 0x168 (360; STILL CURRENT),
move_id 0x528 (1320; fork candidate — validated behaviorally), damage_taken 0x1260 (4704; HP is
ENCRYPTED on T8, so health is computed = round_start_health - damage_taken). `round_gate_offset`
0x15C0 (5568) is frames_since_round_start (0 during intro). `jin_char_id` 6 and kazuya 12 are
the round-start oracle: unlike --base-scan (which DISCOVERS Jin's id), the current sources state
it, so it is validated. The state-word offsets, the legacy booleans they supersede, the
transform component scan, and the global/match anchor are REUSED from base_scan / global_scan
(shared DATA). If a patch moves the storing function, re-source aob_pattern from a current tool
and re-run — that is the only maintenance the AoB approach needs (it is self-healing otherwise).
